bins.name: Integrated joint study required
bins.desc: Bloom strongly supports studying load and co-located generation together as a composite resource. The current bifurcated approach overstates upgrade needs; an integrated study can cut costs by billions and speed interconnection.
quotes: The ANOPR presents a major leap forward for interconnecting on-site generation and load as a composite resource.  The Commission should act expeditiously in adopting interconnection reforms.

Bloom takes no position on whether the Federal Power Act provides FERC jurisdiction over large load interconnections.  However, if the Commission declines to assert jurisdiction, Bloom urges the Commission to adopt reforms to the large generator interconnection procedures for interconnecting on-site large load – that is, new generation co-located with large load behind the same point of interconnection ("hybrid facilities").

Like the ANOPR, Brattle finds that an integrated interconnection study approach for new load and new generation based on requested firm withdrawals and injections can significantly reduce the need for transmission upgrades, potentially avoiding billions in transmission expansion costs.

under existing rules, load is studied as if it seeks firm rights to withdraw 100% from the transmission system to meet its requirements, while the generator is studied as if it seeks to inject 100% of its capacity into the transmission grid, with some RTOs, like PJM, requiring that generator capacity also be studied for deliverability to load throughout the system.  These bifurcated, non-integrated processes ignore on-site supply benefits and can improperly identify required Network Upgrades, resulting in slower interconnection and unnecessarily increasing Network Upgrade costs.
DOE's proposal makes sense, though the Commission should clarify that such rights refer to "firm" withdrawal and injection rights.

Consistent with existing reliability study requirements for Surplus Interconnection Service, a 90-day period to study hybrid facilities seems reasonable, provided requested withdrawal and injection rights do not identify the need for additional reliability studies to determine if Network Upgrades are required.

bins.name: Network upgrade cost protection
bins.desc: Bloom supports assigning 100% of upgrade costs tied to generator injections to hybrid facility generators, consistent with existing policy. However, it opposes any rule denying cash-equivalent refunds, calling such a rule unduly discriminatory and a deterrent to needed generation.
quotes: Properly structured hybrid facility interconnection procedures will incentivize data centers to deploy on-site generation that meets their peak loads, thereby easing resource adequacy concerns while protecting existing customers from costs.

Like the ANOPR, Brattle finds that an integrated interconnection study approach for new load and new generation based on requested firm withdrawals and injections can significantly reduce the need for transmission upgrades, potentially avoiding billions in transmission expansion costs.

the proposal for hybrid facility generators to be responsible for 100% of identified Network Upgrades associated with their requested injections but potentially without cash equivalent refunds should be rejected.  There is no reason to treat cash equivalent refunds for such Network Upgrades differently from other generators that are subject to the Commission's pro forma rules or RTO rules.
Treating a hybrid facility generator differently than other generation subject to FERC jurisdiction would be unduly discriminatory.
Given these benefits, the Commission should decline to adopt rules that could dissuade much needed generation to serve large load by creating unduly discriminatory cash equivalent refund rules.

bins.name: Co-location benefits ratepayers
bins.desc: Bloom argues that properly structured co-location rules incentivize data centers to bring their own generation, easing resource adequacy strain and shielding existing customers from costs.
quotes: Properly structured hybrid facility interconnection procedures will incentivize data centers to deploy on-site generation that meets their peak loads, thereby easing resource adequacy concerns while protecting existing customers from costs.

bins.name: Broaden expedited study access
bins.desc: Bloom supports expedited studies but opposes limiting them to curtailable and dispatchable facilities. Any hybrid facility where primary power covers 100% of peak load should also qualify, because network upgrades are unlikely in that case.
quotes: Bloom urges the Commission to expand expedited studies to include hybrid facilities having primary power sufficient to meet its peak load.

The ANOPR seemingly takes the position that agreement by a hybrid facility to be dispatchable and curtailable is the "grand bargain" for expedited interconnection studies, but it should not be the only way to receive expedited studies.

bins.name: Jurisdiction agnosticism
bins.desc: Bloom takes no position on whether FPA gives FERC jurisdiction over large load interconnections, but asks for generator-side interconnection reforms regardless of the jurisdictional outcome.
quotes: Bloom takes no position on whether the Federal Power Act provides FERC jurisdiction over large load interconnections.  However, if the Commission declines to assert jurisdiction, Bloom urges the Commission to adopt reforms to the large generator interconnection procedures for interconnecting on-site large load – that is, new generation co-located with large load behind the same point of interconnection ("hybrid facilities").

bins.name: Joint load-generation study
bins.desc: Bloom backs the ANOPR proposal to study large load and co-located generation together, citing Brattle analysis showing potential to avoid billions in transmission costs.
quotes: Like the ANOPR, Brattle finds that an integrated interconnection study approach for new load and new generation based on requested firm withdrawals and injections can significantly reduce the need for transmission upgrades, potentially avoiding billions in transmission expansion costs.

under existing rules, load is studied as if it seeks firm rights to withdraw 100% from the transmission system to meet its requirements, while the generator is studied as if it seeks to inject 100% of its capacity into the transmission grid, with some RTOs, like PJM, requiring that generator capacity also be studied for deliverability to load throughout the system.  These bifurcated, non-integrated processes ignore on-site supply benefits and can improperly identify required Network Upgrades, resulting in slower interconnection and unnecessarily increasing Network Upgrade costs.

bins.name: Clarify firm injection/withdrawal rights
bins.desc: Bloom supports studying hybrid facilities based on requested rights, but asks FERC to specify that those rights are firm, not non-firm.
quotes: DOE's proposal makes sense, though the Commission should clarify that such rights refer to "firm" withdrawal and injection rights.

bins.name: Protection equipment and penalties
bins.desc: Bloom supports requiring system protection equipment but urges FERC to define unauthorized injections narrowly. Penalties should apply only to intentional, sustained non-compliance, not to unavoidable transient flows. Technical minimums should align with IEEE 1547-2018 and NERC IBR guidelines.
quotes: The scope of the term "unauthorized" must be addressed.  Bloom urges the Commission to distinguish between inadvertent or transient injections (authorized injections), and sustained or intentional injections that result in malfunction or non-compliance with transmission provider or RTO directives (unauthorized injections).

Any minimum technical requirements should be consistent with existing standards, such as IEEE 1547-2018 and emerging NERC IBR performance and modeling guidelines to avoid duplicative or conflicting requirements.

bins.name: Expand expedited study eligibility
bins.desc: Bloom supports expedited interconnection studies and a 90-day timeline. It urges FERC to extend expedited access beyond curtailable/dispatchable facilities to those with primary power sufficient to cover full peak load.
quotes: The ANOPR presents a major leap forward for interconnecting on-site generation and load as a composite resource.  The Commission should act expeditiously in adopting interconnection reforms.
Bloom urges the Commission to expand expedited studies to include hybrid facilities having primary power sufficient to meet its peak load.

The ANOPR seemingly takes the position that agreement by a hybrid facility to be dispatchable and curtailable is the "grand bargain" for expedited interconnection studies, but it should not be the only way to receive expedited studies.

Consistent with existing reliability study requirements for Surplus Interconnection Service, a 90-day period to study hybrid facilities seems reasonable, provided requested withdrawal and injection rights do not identify the need for additional reliability studies to determine if Network Upgrades are required.

bins.name: Retain cash-equivalent refunds
bins.desc: Bloom opposes any ANOPR rule that would make hybrid facility generators responsible for 100% of network upgrade costs without cash-equivalent refunds. Order No. 2003 established refunds as a financing mechanism, not a rate; removing them for hybrid generators would be unduly discriminatory.
quotes: the proposal for hybrid facility generators to be responsible for 100% of identified Network Upgrades associated with their requested injections but potentially without cash equivalent refunds should be rejected.  There is no reason to treat cash equivalent refunds for such Network Upgrades differently from other generators that are subject to the Commission's pro forma rules or RTO rules.
Treating a hybrid facility generator differently than other generation subject to FERC jurisdiction would be unduly discriminatory.
Given these benefits, the Commission should decline to adopt rules that could dissuade much needed generation to serve large load by creating unduly discriminatory cash equivalent refund rules.

bins.name: Align protection standards with IEEE/NERC
bins.desc: Bloom asks FERC to require that any minimum technical protection requirements align with IEEE 1547-2018 and emerging NERC IBR guidelines, so hybrid facility generators are not subject to duplicative or conflicting obligations.
quotes: The scope of the term "unauthorized" must be addressed.  Bloom urges the Commission to distinguish between inadvertent or transient injections (authorized injections), and sustained or intentional injections that result in malfunction or non-compliance with transmission provider or RTO directives (unauthorized injections).

Any minimum technical requirements should be consistent with existing standards, such as IEEE 1547-2018 and emerging NERC IBR performance and modeling guidelines to avoid duplicative or conflicting requirements.

bins.name: Non-discriminatory treatment for hybrid generators
bins.desc: Bloom argues that hybrid facility generators are FERC-jurisdictional resources and must be treated the same as other generators for cash-equivalent refund purposes. Singling them out for worse treatment would be unduly discriminatory.
quotes: Treating a hybrid facility generator differently than other generation subject to FERC jurisdiction would be unduly discriminatory.
Given these benefits, the Commission should decline to adopt rules that could dissuade much needed generation to serve large load by creating unduly discriminatory cash equivalent refund rules.

bins.name: PJM bifurcated study problem
bins.desc: Bloom uses PJM as the example of a region where current bifurcated study processes ignore co-located supply benefits and improperly identify required network upgrades.
quotes: under existing rules, load is studied as if it seeks firm rights to withdraw 100% from the transmission system to meet its requirements, while the generator is studied as if it seeks to inject 100% of its capacity into the transmission grid, with some RTOs, like PJM, requiring that generator capacity also be studied for deliverability to load throughout the system.  These bifurcated, non-integrated processes ignore on-site supply benefits and can improperly identify required Network Upgrades, resulting in slower interconnection and unnecessarily increasing Network Upgrade costs.
